content:
7 min read
Just now

--

I expected AI to find bugs. Instead, it exposed years of engineering habits, architectural drift, and technical debt that our team had slowly become blind to.
If you’ve been writing C# for a decade or more, you probably trust your instincts during code reviews.
I did too.
After 18+ years building enterprise .NET applications, I believed I knew exactly where problems usually hide.
Slow SQL queries.
Incorrect async usage.
Dependency Injection mistakes.
Memory leaks.
Race conditions.
Then I decided to run an AI-powered review across an enterprise solution containing more than 100,000 lines of C# code.
I expected a few useful suggestions.
Instead, I discovered problems that had quietly accumulated over years — problems no single developer, including me, had fully noticed.
This wasn’t about AI replacing developers.
It was about AI revealing patterns humans struggle to see across an entire codebase.
The Experiment
The project wasn’t a demo application.
It was a production enterprise solution consisting of:

• ASP.NET Core Web APIs

• Background Workers
